items.title: Most common cause of primary hypothyroidism
items.contentHtml:
Hashimoto's thyroiditis

  • Hashimoto's thyroiditis is the most common cause of primary hypothyroidism in areas with iodine sufficiency; It is an autoimmune disease with lymphocytic infiltration and antithyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) and antithyroglobulin antibodies.
  • The presence of anti-TPO antibodies is detected in >90% of cases and confirms the etiological diagnosis, although it is not necessary to initiate treatment if there is biochemical hypothyroidism.
  • Ultrasound shows a heterogeneous parenchyma, hypoechoic and with a micronodular (“honeycomb”) pattern.
  • It is more common in women (8:1) and can be associated with other autoimmune diseases such as type 1 diabetes mellitus or Addison's disease.

items.foco: Elevated TSH and low FT4 in primary hypothyroidism
items.title: Biochemical profile of primary hypothyroidism
items.contentHtml:
Primary hypothyroidism: high TSH and low FT4

  • In primary hypothyroidism, the thyroid gland fails directly; the pituitary compensates by secreting more thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), which is why elevated TSH and low free thyroxine (FT4) are observed.
  • It is the most common pattern in clinical practice (>95% of hypothyroidism cases).
  • The differential diagnosis includes secondary (central) hypothyroidism, where the TSH may be inappropriately normal or low and the FT4 low; If suspected, also request free T3 and adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) to rule out panhypopituitarism.
  • Examination pitfall: in severe systemic disease (sick euthyroid syndrome) TSH may be low or normal with low FT4, but the patient does not have primary hypothyroidism.

items.foco: Subclinical hypothyroidism: elevated TSH, normal FT4
items.title: Definition and management of subclinical hypothyroidism
items.contentHtml:
Subclinical hypothyroidism

  • It is defined by elevated TSH (generally between 4.5-10 mIU/L) with normal free thyroxine (FT4).
  • It is a common finding, especially in women over 60 years of age; Progression to frank hypothyroidism occurs at 2-5% annually if anti-TPO antibodies are positive.
  • Treatment with levothyroxine is indicated if: TSH >10 mIU/L, or TSH between 4.5-10 mIU/L with clear symptoms, pregnancy, goiter, positive anti-TPO antibodies or dyslipidemia.
  • Do not treat if TSH <10 mIU/L in asymptomatic patients, without goiter or antibodies, especially in those over 70 years of age (risk of atrial fibrillation and osteoporosis).

items.title: Cardinal symptoms of hypothyroidism
items.contentHtml:
Classic symptoms of hypothyroidism

  • The most characteristic symptoms are fatigue, cold intolerance and constipation, due to general metabolic slowdown.
  • Other common symptoms: dry skin, hair loss, brittle nails, hoarseness, bradypsychia, depression, weight gain (modest, usually <5 kg), myalgias and arthralgias.
  • Menstrual irregularity (oligomenorrhea or menorrhagia) and infertility may occur in women.
  • Examination pitfall: weight loss is not typical of hypothyroidism; If there is weight loss, think about hyperthyroidism or concomitant disease.

items.title: Woltman sign: prolonged Achilles reflex
items.contentHtml:
Achillian reflex with prolonged relaxation

  • The Achillian reflex with prolonged relaxation (Woltman sign) is a classic semiological finding of hypothyroidism; It is explored by percussing the Achilles tendon and observing the relaxation phase, which lasts longer than normal (>300 ms).
  • Physiopathology: thyroid hormone deficiency slows calcium reuptake in the muscular sarcoplasmic reticulum, prolonging contraction-relaxation.
  • It is not pathognomonic (it can be seen in diabetes mellitus, peripheral neuropathies or lithium use), but its presence supports the clinical diagnosis.
  • On examination there may also be bradycardia, cold and dry skin, myxedema and goiter (if the cause is Hashimoto's).

items.foco: Myxedema: periorbital edema and does not leave a pit
items.title: Myxedema: periorbital non-pitting edema
items.contentHtml:
Myxedema in hypothyroidism

  • Myxedema is an edema of firm consistency, which does not leave a pit when pressed, typically located in the periorbital region, back of the hands and feet, and pretibial area.
  • It is produced by the accumulation of glycosaminoglycans (hyaluronic acid) in the dermis, which retain water and give a pasty appearance. and infiltrated.
  • Periorbital edema is an early and characteristic sign; Unlike renal or cardiac edema, it is neither soft nor depressible.
  • In pretibial myxedema (specific for Graves-Basedow disease, not hypothyroidism) it is also non-pitting, but is associated with hyperthyroidism.

items.title: Myxedema coma: hypothyroid emergency
items.contentHtml:
Myxedema coma

  • It is the most severe form of hypothyroidism, with mortality of 20-50%; It is usually triggered by infection, cold, sedatives or surgery in a patient with untreated hypothyroidism.
  • Clinical triad: hypothermia (temperature <35°C, without chills), hypoventilation (hypoxia and hypercapnia) and bradycardia.
  • Other findings: hypotension, generalized myxedema, confusion or coma, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia (due to inadequate secretion of antidiuretic hormone).
  • Treatment: intravenous levothyroxine (loading dose 200-400 mcg, then 1.6 mcg/kg/day) plus hydrocortisone (50-100 mg every 6-8 hours) until adrenal insufficiency is ruled out; ventilatory support and passive rewarming.

items.title: Initial dose of levothyroxine in hypothyroidism
items.contentHtml:
Replacement therapy with levothyroxine

  • The treatment of choice is levothyroxine (synthetic T4), with a usual initial dose of 1.6 mcg/kg/day (approximately 100-150 mcg/day in adults).
  • In patients over 65 years of age or with ischemic heart disease, start with a low dose. (25-50 mcg/day) and gradually increase every 4-6 weeks to avoid myocardial ischemia or arrhythmias.
  • The dose is adjusted according to TSH levels, with the goal of normal TSH (0.5-2.5 mIU/L in young adults; 4-6 mIU/L in the elderly).
  • Take on an empty stomach (30-60 minutes before breakfast) with water, without other medications or supplements that interfere.

items.title: Management of levothyroxine in pregnancy
items.contentHtml:
Levothyroxine in pregnancy

  • During pregnancy, levothyroxine requirements increase by 30-50% from the first trimester due to the increase in thyroxine binding globulin (TBG) and the passage of hormones to the fetus.
  • It is recommended to increase the dose as soon as pregnancy is confirmed (for example, add 2 extra doses a week or increase 30% the daily dose).
  • TSH control every 4-6 weeks; target: TSH <2.5 mIU/L in the first trimester and <3.0 mIU/L in the second and third trimester.
  • Untreated hypothyroidism during pregnancy is associated with abortion, preeclampsia, low birth weight and fetal cognitive impairment.

items.foco: Interaction: levothyroxine with calcium, iron and omeprazole
items.title: Levothyroxine Drug Interactions
items.contentHtml:
Levothyroxine Interactions

  • Levothyroxine has significant interactions with calcium, iron and omeprazole (and other proton pump inhibitors), which reduce their intestinal absorption.
  • Levothyroxine should be administered at least 4 hours before or after calcium or iron supplements, and with omeprazole is recommended to separate the intake by 4-6 hours or switch to an H2 antagonist if possible.
  • Other interactions: cholestyramine, sucralfate, antacids with aluminum/magnesium, raloxifene and orlistat also decrease absorption.
  • Estrogens (oral contraceptives, hormone therapy) increase TBG, requiring a higher dose of levothyroxine.

items.foco: Neonatal screening with TSH in heel
items.title: Neonatal screening for congenital hypothyroidism
items.contentHtml:
Neonatal screening with heel TSH

  • Neonatal screening for congenital hypothyroidism is performed by measuring TSH in heel blood (heel test) between the 2nd and 5th day of life, ideally before hospital discharge.
  • A TSH value >20 mIU/L in heel sample is considered positive. and requires confirmation with TSH and serum free thyroxine (FT4).
  • Congenital hypothyroidism has an incidence of 1:3000-4000 newborns; the most common cause is thyroid dysgenesis (agenesis, ectopia or hypoplasia).
  • Early treatment with levothyroxine (initial dose 10-15 mcg/kg/day) within the first 2 weeks of life prevents irreversible neurological damage (cretinism).

items.title: Thyroid hormone resistance syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Resistance to thyroid hormones

  • The resistance to thyroid hormone syndrome (RTH) is an autosomal dominant genetic disorder due to a mutation in the thyroid hormone beta receptor gene (THRB), which causes tissue insensitivity.
  • Characteristic biochemical profile: normal or elevated TSH with free thyroxine (FT4) and free triiodothyronine (FT3) elevated; the patient is usually euthyroid or with mild symptoms of hypo/hyperthyroidism depending on the tissue.
  • Key differential diagnosis: TSH-secreting pituitary tumor (TSHoma); in RTH the response to thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) is normal and there is no pituitary lesion on MRI.
  • No treatment is required if the patient is euthyroid; if symptoms are present, beta-blockers or exogenous triiodothyronine (T3) are used in selected cases.
